content.problemStatement:
Chef has a permutation AAA of length NNN. Chef can perform the following operation on AAA: \\
**1)** Select LLL and RRR such that (1≤L≤R≤N)(1 \le L \le R \le N)(1≤L≤R≤N) and (R−L+1<N)(R - L + 1 \lt N)(R−L+1<N) \\
**2)** Remove the subarray AL…RA_{L \ldots R}AL…R​ from AAA. (The remaining parts of AAA are concatenated)

The score of this operation === length of the subarray removed =R−L+1 = R - L + 1=R−L+1

For e.g., if A=[3,1,4,6,5,2]A = [3, 1, 4, 6, 5, 2]A=[3,1,4,6,5,2], and Chef selects L=3,R=5L = 3, R = 5L=3,R=5, then the score of this operation =3 = 3=3 and AAA becomes [3,1,2][3, 1, 2][3,1,2].

Chef wants to perform this operation **exactly once** such that, after the operation, AAA still remains a permutation. What is the maximum score that Chef can achieve in doing so?

As a reminder, a permutation of length MMM is an array where every integer from 111 to MMM occurs exactly once.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the permutation AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the permutation AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the maximum score that Chef can achieve after applying the operation exactly once such that AAA still remains a permutation.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 5·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N (2 ≤ N ≤ 10^5), the size of the permutation. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1,A2,…,AN – a permutation of 1..N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single integer – the maximum possible length of the removed subarray (the score).

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: only the element 3 (value N) can be removed, giving score 1.
Test case 2: the suffix {2,3,4,5,6,7} occupies positions 2..7, so length 6 can be removed.
Test case 3: values {4,5,6} are at positions 3..5, giving maximum score 3.
Removing the single element 2 leaves [1], which is a permutation of size 1.
All numbers 2..15 form a contiguous block, so the longest removable subarray has length N‑1 = 14.

solution.approach: For a removal to leave a permutation, the remaining numbers must be exactly {1,…,N‑k} where k is the length of the removed subarray. Hence the removed subarray must contain precisely the k largest values {N‑k+1,…,N}. Let pos[x] be the index of value x in the original array. For a given k the values N‑k+1…N occupy positions whose minimum is minPos and maximum is maxPos. They form a contiguous segment iff maxPos‑minPos+1 = k. Starting with k=1 (only value N) we keep track of the current minPos and maxPos while we extend the suffix one value at a time (adding N‑k). For each k (1 ≤ k < N) we update minPos = min(minPos, pos[N‑k]) and maxPos = max(maxPos, pos[N‑k]). If the interval size equals k, the suffix of size k is contiguous and can be removed. The answer is the largest such k. The scan is O(N) per test case and uses O(N) extra memory for the position array.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, total O(ΣN) ≤ 5·10^5

code.Python3:
import sys
def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it = iter(data)
    T = next(it)
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = next(it)
        pos = [0]*(N+1)
        for i in range(1,N+1):
            x = next(it)
            pos[x]=i
        minPos = maxPos = pos[N]
        best = 1
        for k in range(2,N):
            val = N - k + 1
            p = pos[val]
            if p < minPos: minPos = p
            if p > maxPos: maxPos = p
            if maxPos - minPos + 1 == k:
                best = k
        out.append(str(best))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
